name: Ganesha Purana
shortDescription: A Hindu scripture dedicated to Ganesha, exploring his origins, deeds, and significance as the supreme Brahman.
description: The Ganesha Purana is one of the major Puranas in Hinduism, dedicated to the elephant-headed god Ganesha. It is an important text for devotees of Ganesha, known as Ganapatyas, and serves as a primary source for understanding his mythology, theology, and worship. The Purana is divided into two sections: the Upasanakhanda (section on worship) and the Kridakhanda (section on sports/activities). The Upasanakhanda focuses on Ganesha's role as the supreme Brahman and provides detailed instructions on his worship, mantras, and rituals. The Kridakhanda narrates various stories about Ganesha's birth, childhood, battles, and interactions with other deities and beings. It establishes Ganesha as the foremost deity, the remover of obstacles, and the bestower of success and wisdom. The text emphasizes Ganesha's benevolent nature and his accessibility to devotees, making him a widely revered deity in Hinduism. The Ganesha Purana played a significant role in popularizing Ganesha worship and shaping his image as a compassionate and powerful god.
subtitle: The Sacred Chronicle of the Elephant-Headed God
author: Traditional; attributed to Vyasa
period: Estimated between the 11th and 14th centuries CE
significance: A foundational text for Ganapatya sect, shaping Ganesha's modern image and worship.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The Ganesha Purana narrates several accounts of Ganesha's birth, often involving Parvati, Shiva's consort, creating him from her own bodily impurities. Another version describes Ganesha emerging spontaneously from the cosmic sound 'Om'. The Kridakhanda details Ganesha's childhood adventures, his battles with demons like Sindhu, and his role in establishing dharma. Key myths include his acquisition of the elephant head, usually associated with Shiva beheading him and later replacing it with an elephant's head, and his broken tusk, which he used to write the Mahabharata as dictated by Vyasa. These narratives establish Ganesha's divine status and his association with wisdom, strength, and auspiciousness.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: The Ganesha Purana serves as a guide for Ganesha worship, providing detailed instructions on rituals, mantras, and offerings. It emphasizes Ganesha's role as the remover of obstacles (Vighnaharta) and the bestower of success (Siddhidata). Devotees believe that worshipping Ganesha can bring good fortune, wisdom, and protection from negative influences. The Purana's influence is evident in the widespread popularity of Ganesha worship across India and beyond. It promotes the idea that Ganesha is easily accessible to devotees, regardless of their social status or background, making him a beloved deity for people from all walks of life.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Ganesha's elephant head symbolizes wisdom, intellect, and discrimination. His large ears represent his ability to listen to the prayers of his devotees, and his trunk signifies adaptability and efficiency. The modak (sweet dumpling) he often holds represents the sweetness of liberation and the rewards of spiritual practice. His single tusk symbolizes the ability to overcome duality and focus on the ultimate truth. The Ganesha Purana encourages devotees to interpret these symbols and apply them to their own lives, striving for wisdom, self-control, and spiritual growth.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The Ganesha Purana has had a lasting impact on Hindu culture and religious practices. It has shaped the iconography of Ganesha and influenced the development of various Ganesha temples and festivals. The text continues to be studied and recited by devotees, scholars, and priests, preserving its teachings for future generations. Ganesha's image as a benevolent and accessible deity has resonated with people across different cultures and backgrounds, making him a popular figure in art, literature, and popular culture. His role as the remover of obstacles has made him a symbol of hope and new beginnings.

sources.author: Winternitz, Maurice
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Indian literature, including the Puranas.

sources.author: Thapan, Anita Raina
sources.work: Understanding Ganapati: Insights into the History, Mythology and Ritual of India's Most Popular God
sources.description: A detailed study of Ganesha, his mythology, and his worship.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Associated with purity, cleansing, and the flow of blessings.

associations.name: Mouse/Shrew
associations.significance: Ganesha's mount, representing control over desires and overcoming obstacles.

associations.name: Modak
associations.significance: Represents sweetness, reward, and spiritual fulfillment.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Janus
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are associated with beginnings, transitions, and overcoming obstacles.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Hermes
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are associated with wisdom, communication, and good fortune.

companions.name: Parvati
companions.description: Ganesha's mother, the goddess of power, love, and motherhood.

companions.name: Shiva
companions.description: Ganesha's father, the god of destruction and transformation.

companions.name: Kartikeya
companions.description: Ganesha's brother, the god of war and victory.

worshipPractices.name: Puja
worshipPractices.description: Elaborate rituals involving offerings, prayers, and mantras.

worshipPractices.name: Mantra Recitation
worshipPractices.description: Chanting specific mantras dedicated to Ganesha for blessings and protection.

festivals.name: Ganesh Chaturthi
festivals.description: A ten-day festival celebrating Ganesha's birth, marked by elaborate processions and idol installations.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ganesha remains a widely worshipped deity, appearing in art, movies, and businesses as a symbol of good luck and new beginnings. His image is used extensively in advertising and branding.
